title: Annos international press conference
wikitext:
[]: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by FelipeFritschF » Sun Sep 05, 2021 9:18 pm I'm making this thread because this seems to be the same interview Anno gave to other international outlets. It received some considerable attention as reported by NYT and Collider , but this interview seems to be the most complete account of it. In it, he talks about Shin Eva, the production of NTE, Shinji's development, and his plans for the franchise. Unlike the other articles, this includes little in the way of paraphrasing, though I had to translate it from the Portuguese. This is my transcript and translation of a video posted by Brazilian media outlet Omelete (it means what you think it means) on August 25th. It was made private just a couple of hours later, but a friend was able to download the video and has re-uploaded it here . Enjoy. [Introduction, summary on the Rebuilds and comment on NGE's social phenomenom. Interview starts at 04:44] Moo (host): And knowing all of that, of all this consolidated relevance of the original work, a lot of people asked themselves why the director wanted to work in Evangelion again, and Hideaki Anno awnsered that for us. Anno [04:55]: What led me to re-do Evangelion was the fact that it was the only thing I could do at the time. I did try to create other things, but none of what I did or planned was anywhere as good as Evangelion.[1] At the end, I reached the conclusion that creating Evangelion was more fun. I only like to do what is more interesting at the moment. That is why I made the choice to make Evangelion again. And, by deciding to make it again, I did not want it to have the same ending, no matter what. Time passed, the world had changed, I myself had changed a litle, and I did not think that having the same ending would make sense. I thought I could have a more interesting ending and I searched that for a long time, which led me to this ending. In this way, I did everything that was within my reach and I am satisfied. [Comments by the hosts on changing themes of the franchise] Kitsune (translator and "authority on Eva"): [07:00] ...and that is something me and Moo wanted to know about Hideaki Anno and this new Evangelion movie. Why and how does Shinji change? Is this the same Shinji as the one from the '95 series and the film End of Evangelion? Is it a new Shinji? Is it a continuation of Shinji? We also had the opportunity to ask him this. Anno [07:23]: At the time of the TV series and the film, Ikari Shinji could only talk about himself, and try to save himself, at any cost. This time, he became an adult, evolved, and he can save the people around him as well. He was able to grow up to this point. I think that is the great difference. Before, he was only a child. [They also brought in Fábio Lucindo, Shinji's Brazilian VA since the first Brazilian dub in 1999. He also served as director for Netflix's redub of the series and Prime Video's redub of NTE. Interview with him at the time here . Lucindo recounts how he first heavily identified with Shinji when first dubbing him, but over time started to see him as annoying and cowardly. Yet, later on, he understood that he was "just a teenager full of problems". Lucindo was 15 at the time. Asuka's VA, Fernanda Bullara, was 14.] Moo [08:56]: ...and as Hideaki Anno also commented, which blew my mind, the conclusion of the story is another. Because here, in the Rebuilds, Shinji is able to face several personal matters he has, and that is what makes a change and contributes to the update of the context of Evangelion, which dialogues more with the Japan of today as well, which has debated more these social and familial relations, and the individualiy of the Japanese person inside this collective. So just like the original work was influenced by its context, the current context has also influenced this version. Anno [09:25]: Previously, in the TV series and film, we couldn't explore much the father figure of Gendo. We were not able to depict him. This time, when I tried to recreate Evangelion, since the beginning, I had decided I'd explore the relationship of father and son. Therefore, this time, I made an effort to better depict the "father". Moo [10:00]: [comments on religious and sci-fi references]... and the director has also commented a little on his perspective on how people have different interpretations and whether the emotional load of the characters ends up helping for a better understanding of the work. Anno [11:08]: The misconceptions and misunderstandings were foreseen at the time of creation. At the time, I did everything I could for it to be minimized, but each person interprets it their own way, and inconsitencies end up appearing. This difference between what I try to transmit and what is understood is inevitable. I tried to make it so that this difference would be minimal but, because everything depens on each person, it gets very hard. Someone who lives in a similar enviroment to that of the protagonist can interpret what I tried to transmit in a better way, I think. But I did not intend to create only for those people. I created trying to include the feeling[s] of people that see people like that.[2] I created this work with the intent of transmitting various things to diverse people. [Kitsune makes more comments on Eva as a whole, and notes that Omelete was part of an international press conference.[3]] Kitsune [14:30]: ...and he was asked about this, about the reach of Evangelion in the world: Anno [14:33]: Those who create is are always thinking of what could be interesting, of what would be fun. Me and the crew have the role of searching that which is interesting. I searched everything that was instigating and, thinking how to make it even more interesing, I continued to create, which resulted in Evangelion. I put everything I found interesting in Evangelion. When all this content reached the viewer, it could be comprehended by several/diverse people, by a lot of people. I didn't expect that to happen, that it'd be so succesful all around the world. Basically, my works are in Japanese, based on Japanese culture. I only thought that, if it pleased people beyond this border, I'd be very grateful. I hadn't thought about it. Even so, people all around the world could enjoy it. It was unpredictable, and I was glad. I am very grateful. Animations are a creation, a world of pure fiction. I think it is the most adequate manner to substitute image in video, the best method, in my opinion. And, to substitute this image, even with words, even though there the language barrier exists, animation is able to overcome this obstacle. In comparison with other media, it presents few problems. Because of that, I think animation is the format most appreciated all aroun the world. In this sense, I think that more animations should be created all around the world. In terms of age range, I think not only cartoons, but even animation for the adult public, that are fun for adults, should/will become ever more common all around the world. Let's say... getting more elaborate. Moo [19:25]: ...and besides commenting on how this experience (working on live aciton movies) influenced the Rebuild movies, I could ask him if the more robust let him deliver something more complete in accordance with what he wanted to develop in the story. Anno [19:36]: There is a limit to what we an express through animation. In trying to express something impossible in animation, it is necessary to look at techniques like those of live-action, among others. There is no alternative. In relation to that, since the beginning, I created this sort of structure. At that time, the animation technology had finally reached the point of being able to support this sort of structure. The new Evangelion[4] was made this way. But in reality, this was being done for a long time, for over 30 years. It is necessary to inject a lot of money in production, and in this sense, it was possible to make many things thanks to the value invested. Pre-visualization, created with CGI, in which we assemble the images and then transform them in hand-drawn animation, is not at all common. This time, thanks to Evangelion's prestige, I did what I couldn't do before. Being able to do all of this made me very happy. Certainly, thanks to all the people that supported us, it was possible to have a little more funding. I also think that they thought it was made in a very technological manner. By the way, you look very beautiful in the Ayanami cosplay. [host explains she was wearing it at the time. Kitsune talks a bit about production problems during NGE and EoE, and Shin Godzilla. He concludes saying that "art is also [having] time [and not only money and resources], and that is something Anno was able to give himself during production of Rebuild of Evangelion"] Anno [25:08]: In the previous time, even the creation of Evangelion Q, which came before, I was able to stand it. But, after finishing Evangelion Q, I had dedicated myself too much, in excess. I couldn't continue with Evangelion, I'd no longer have psychological of physical conditions to move forward. In Japanese, there is a modern expression, "Kokoroga oreru", which means "with the shattered soul", and I felt that way. To re-establish myself, I worked in another project, and that did a lot of good to me. I was able to gain the strenght to return to Evangelion, thanks to this different enviroment. If it wasn't for this interval, I think Evangelion would still not be done. Almost everything one can do in animation nowadays was done in this Evangelion. For some time, for some years, I pretend to create my works in live-action. In relation to the big productions, I intend to make them when I'm able to think of an adequate project. In the case of a big producion, it is hard to make it a reality. In Japan, it isn't very easy to get funding. It's complicated. Even if I wanted to, well, if the opportunity presents itself, I'd like to make it. As long as I'm young. I mean, as long as I'm in my 70s.[5] Making the new version was plenty of fun, but it was stressful, because it had to be different from the previous ones. Besides that, every viewer, when it comes to Evangelion, has their own vision of the work, and trying not to destroy those visions and keep the work interesting at the same time was exhaustive work. It was like that. In relation to making Evangelion one more time, I do not have that pretension at the moment. Not currently. But I can't say there is no chance. But when it comes to this 14-year gap in the narrative, if one day I have the chance to talk about it, I'd like to do it, yes. However, I believe there will not be a continuation of this film. [Moo believes it might happen soon, perhaps with other characters like Asuka (while cosplaying as her). Lucindo says he thinks the movie is supposed to provide closure, but he does expect to happen eventually also. Kitsune goes on about the movie being so finate, but thinks he's setting himself up for dissapointment. ] Notes: First of all, this is a double translation from Anno's Japanese wording, then translated into Portuguese by Omelete, and then to English by myself, as well as their questions, so do keep in mind this might not be exact wording Anno used. [1] lit. "came close to the feet of Evangelion": unrivaled, there was no comparison, it wasn't the same, not as good. I'm not sure if Anno is outright saying his other works (which I do enjoy) weren't as good in quality or if maybe there was just something missing to him on a personal, artistic, or cultural/industry impact level. [2] lit. people like that/see people that *in that way/manner* [3] This is probably the same one that included correspondents from NYT and Collider. I'm wonder how many other international outlets might have informaion from it that we have not seen yet. [4] I'm pretty sure Anno is saying literally "Shin Evangelion", refering to the movie itself. [5]The wording here is weird. I don't know if Anno means to say he'll wait until he's 70 to take a look at it, or the 70 age range, or if that's just his upper limit when he still considers himself to be young.

[]: Re: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Sun Sep 05, 2021 10:33 pm Thank you! One thing I'm a bit confused on the wording of: "Someone who lives in a similar enviroment to that of the protagonist can interpret what I tried to transmit in a better way, I think. But I did not intend to create only for those people. I created trying to include the feeling[s] of people that see people like that.[2] I created this work with the intent of transmitting various things to diverse people." Is this saying that he intended to make something relatable both to people who see themselves in Shinji, and those who don't?

[]: Re: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by FelipeFritschF » Sun Sep 05, 2021 11:06 pm I had that doubt myself. The wording used is pretty weird, but I think it could be both. "Mas não tive a intenção de criar apenas para essas pessoas. Criei tentando incluir o sentimento de pessoas que veem gente assim. Criei essa obra com o intuito de transmitir várias coisas para diversas pessoas." "the feelings of people who see people like that" can also apply to those on, shall we say, the receiving end of people like Shinji. Strictly speaking, even "diversas" can be translated simply as various, as in number, but not necessarily diverse.

[]: Re: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by RussianRiz » Sun Sep 05, 2021 11:57 pm In the conversation Anno has with Utada, he explains that he wanted to convey Shinji's journey so that people experiencing depression could identify, but also those who didn't to understand the feeling. I believe Anno is repeating the message in this interview. It is both an internal and an external view. Not just for him, but for people who may or may not identify with Shinji. https://youtu.be/gOCMSUbsG-8?t=337 "I want people who have experienced depression to emphatize, and I also want people who don't know about depression to understand what it's like."

[]: Re: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Mon Sep 06, 2021 7:21 am FelipeFritschF wrote: "the feelings of people who see people like that" can also apply to those on, shall we say, the receiving end of people like Shinji.

[]: Re: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by Tlalac » Tue Sep 07, 2021 4:40 am FelipeFritschF wrote: But when it comes to this 14-year gap in the narrative, if one day I have the chance to talk about it, I'd like to do it, yes.

[]: Re: Anno's international press conference             Quote Post by Archer » Tue Sep 07, 2021 7:46 am Tlalac wrote: yes please !
